question:
Suppose that a positive integer $N$ can be expressed as the sum of $k$ consecutive positive integers
\[
N = a + (a+1) +(a+2) + \cdots + (a+k-1)
\]
for $k=2017$ but for no other values of $k>1$. Considering all positive integers $N$ with this property,
what is the smallest positive integer $a$ that occurs in any of these expressions?
solution:
We prove that the smallest value of $a$ is 16.

Note that the expression for $N$ can be rewritten as $k(2a+k-1)/2$,
so that $2N = k(2a+k-1)$. In this expression, $k>1$ by requirement;
$k < 2a+k-1$ because $a>1$; and obviously $k$ and $2a+k-1$ have opposite parity. Conversely, for any factorization $2N = mn$ with $1<m<n$ and $m,n$ of opposite parity, we obtain an expression of $N$ in the desired form by taking
$k = m$, $a = (n+1-m)/2$.

We now note that $2017$ is prime. (On the exam, solvers would have had to verify this by hand.
Since $2017 < 45^2$, this can be done by trial division by the primes up to 43.)
For $2N = 2017(2a+2016)$ not to have another expression of the specified form, it must be the case that
$2a+2016$ has no odd divisor greater than 1; that is, $2a+2016$ must be a power of 2.
This first occurs for $2a+2016=2048$, yielding the claimed result.

\textbf{Reinterpretation:}
(by Karl Mahlburg)
To avoid $N$ having another representation, for $k = 2, \dots, 2016$, we must have
\[
N \not\equiv \begin{cases} k/2 & k \equiv 0 \pmod{2} \\
0 & k \equiv 1 \pmod{2}.
\end{cases}
\]
Consequently, $N \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$ for any odd prime $p<2017$
and $N \equiv 0 \pmod{1024}$. Since $N$ must be divisible by 2017, this again yields the claimed value of $a$.

descriptive_long.question:
Suppose that a positive integer totalnum can be expressed as the sum of consecnum consecutive positive integers
\[
totalnum = startint + (startint+1) +(startint+2) + \cdots + (startint+consecnum-1)
\]
for consecnum=2017 but for no other values of consecnum>1. Considering all positive integers totalnum with this property,
what is the smallest positive integer startint that occurs in any of these expressions?
descriptive_long.solution:
We prove that the smallest value of startint is 16.

Note that the expression for totalnum can be rewritten as consecnum(2startint+consecnum-1)/2,
so that \(2totalnum = consecnum(2startint+consecnum-1)\). In this expression, consecnum>1 by requirement;
consecnum < 2startint+consecnum-1 because startint>1; and obviously consecnum and 2startint+consecnum-1 have opposite parity. Conversely, for any factorization \(2totalnum = factora\,factorb\) with \(1<factora<factorb\) and factora and factorb of opposite parity, we obtain an expression of totalnum in the desired form by taking
\(consecnum = factora\), \(startint = (factorb+1-factora)/2\).

We now note that 2017 is prime. (On the exam, solvers would have had to verify this by hand.
Since \(2017 < 45^2\), this can be done by trial division by the primes up to 43.)
For \(2totalnum = 2017(2startint+2016)\) not to have another expression of the specified form, it must be the case that
\(2startint+2016\) has no odd divisor greater than 1; that is, \(2startint+2016\) must be a power of 2.
This first occurs for \(2startint+2016=2048\), yielding the claimed result.

\textbf{Reinterpretation:}
(by Karl Mahlburg)
To avoid totalnum having another representation, for consecnum = 2, \dots, 2016, we must have
\[
totalnum \not\equiv \begin{cases} consecnum/2 & consecnum \equiv 0 \pmod{2} \\
0 & consecnum \equiv 1 \pmod{2}.
\end{cases}
\]
Consequently, \(totalnum \not\equiv 0 \pmod{primevar}\) for any odd prime primevar<2017
and \(totalnum \equiv 0 \pmod{1024}\). Since totalnum must be divisible by 2017, this again yields the claimed value of startint.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Suppose that a positive integer $moonlight$ can be expressed as the sum of $windsock$ consecutive positive integers
\[
moonlight = pineapple + (pineapple+1) +(pineapple+2) + \cdots + (pineapple+windsock-1)
\]
for $windsock=2017$ but for no other values of $windsock>1$. Considering all positive integers $moonlight$ with this property,
what is the smallest positive integer $pineapple$ that occurs in any of these expressions?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We prove that the smallest value of $pineapple$ is 16.

Note that the expression for $moonlight$ can be rewritten as $windsock(2pineapple+windsock-1)/2$,
so that $2moonlight = windsock(2pineapple+windsock-1)$. In this expression, $windsock>1$ by requirement;
$windsock < 2pineapple+windsock-1$ because $pineapple>1$; and obviously $windsock$ and $2pineapple+windsock-1$ have opposite parity. Conversely, for any factorization $2moonlight = waterfallhurricane$ with $1<waterfall<hurricane$ and $waterfall,hurricane$ of opposite parity, we obtain an expression of $moonlight$ in the desired form by taking
$windsock = waterfall$, $pineapple = (hurricane+1-waterfall)/2$.

We now note that $2017$ is prime. (On the exam, solvers would have had to verify this by hand.
Since $2017 < 45^2$, this can be done by trial division by the primes up to 43.)
For $2moonlight = 2017(2pineapple+2016)$ not to have another expression of the specified form, it must be the case that
$2pineapple+2016$ has no odd divisor greater than 1; that is, $2pineapple+2016$ must be a power of 2.
This first occurs for $2pineapple+2016=2048$, yielding the claimed result.

\textbf{Reinterpretation:}
(by Karl Mahlburg)
To avoid $moonlight$ having another representation, for $windsock = 2, \dots, 2016$, we must have
\[
moonlight \not\equiv \begin{cases} windsock/2 & windsock \equiv 0 \pmod{2} \\
0 & windsock \equiv 1 \pmod{2}.
\end{cases}
\]
Consequently, $moonlight \not\equiv 0 \pmod{butterfly}$ for any odd prime $butterfly<2017$
and $moonlight \equiv 0 \pmod{1024}$. Since $moonlight$ must be divisible by 2017, this again yields the claimed value of $pineapple$.}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Suppose that a positive integer $nothingness$ can be expressed as the sum of $solitude$ consecutive positive integers
\[
nothingness = terminus + (terminus+1) +(terminus+2) + \cdots + (terminus+solitude-1)
\]
for $solitude=2017$ but for no other values of $solitude>1$. Considering all positive integers $nothingness$ with this property,
what is the smallest positive integer $terminus$ that occurs in any of these expressions?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We prove that the smallest value of $terminus$ is 16.

Note that the expression for $nothingness$ can be rewritten as $solitude(2terminus+solitude-1)/2$, so that $2nothingness = solitude(2terminus+solitude-1)$. In this expression, $solitude>1$ by requirement; $solitude < 2terminus+solitude-1$ because $terminus>1$; and obviously $solitude$ and $2terminus+solitude-1$ have opposite parity. Conversely, for any factorization $2nothingness = colossus\,minuscule$ with $1<colossus<minuscule$ and $colossus,minuscule$ of opposite parity, we obtain an expression of $nothingness$ in the desired form by taking
$solitude = colossus$, $terminus = (minuscule+1-colossus)/2$.

We now note that 2017 is prime. (On the exam, solvers would have had to verify this by hand.
Since 2017 $< 45^2$, this can be done by trial division by the primes up to 43.)
For $2nothingness = 2017(2terminus+2016)$ not to have another expression of the specified form, it must be the case that
$2terminus+2016$ has no odd divisor greater than 1; that is, $2terminus+2016$ must be a power of 2.
This first occurs for $2terminus+2016=2048$, yielding the claimed result.

\textbf{Reinterpretation:}
(by Karl Mahlburg)
To avoid $nothingness$ having another representation, for $solitude = 2, \dots, 2016$, we must have
\[
nothingness \not\equiv \begin{cases} solitude/2 & solitude \equiv 0 \pmod{2} \\ 0 & solitude \equiv 1 \pmod{2}. \end{cases}
\]
Consequently, $nothingness \not\equiv 0 \pmod{composite}$ for any odd prime $composite<2017$
and $nothingness \equiv 0 \pmod{1024}$. Since $nothingness$ must be divisible by 2017, this again yields the claimed value of $terminus$.

garbled_string.question:
Suppose that a positive integer $qzxwvtnp$ can be expressed as the sum of $hjgrksla$ consecutive positive integers
\[
qzxwvtnp = plmdqrfz + (plmdqrfz+1) +(plmdqrfz+2) + \cdots + (plmdqrfz+hjgrksla-1)
\]
for $hjgrksla=2017$ but for no other values of $hjgrksla>1$. Considering all positive integers $qzxwvtnp$ with this property,
what is the smallest positive integer $plmdqrfz$ that occurs in any of these expressions?
garbled_string.solution:
We prove that the smallest value of $plmdqrfz$ is 16.

Note that the expression for $qzxwvtnp$ can be rewritten as $hjgrksla(2plmdqrfz+hjgrksla-1)/2$, so that $2qzxwvtnp = hjgrksla(2plmdqrfz+hjgrksla-1)$. In this expression, $hjgrksla>1$ by requirement; $hjgrksla < 2plmdqrfz+hjgrksla-1$ because $plmdqrfz>1$; and obviously $hjgrksla$ and $2plmdqrfz+hjgrksla-1$ have opposite parity. Conversely, for any factorization $2qzxwvtnp = xcbvneuq trslpgoa$ with $1<xcbvneuq<trslpgoa$ and $xcbvneuq,trslpgoa$ of opposite parity, we obtain an expression of $qzxwvtnp$ in the desired form by taking $hjgrksla = xcbvneuq$, $plmdqrfz = (trslpgoa+1-xcbvneuq)/2$.

We now note that 2017 is prime. (On the exam, solvers would have had to verify this by hand. Since $2017 < 45^2$, this can be done by trial division by the primes up to 43.) For $2qzxwvtnp = 2017(2plmdqrfz+2016)$ not to have another expression of the specified form, it must be the case that $2plmdqrfz+2016$ has no odd divisor greater than 1; that is, $2plmdqrfz+2016$ must be a power of 2. This first occurs for $2plmdqrfz+2016=2048$, yielding the claimed result.

\textbf{Reinterpretation:} (by Karl Mahlburg) To avoid $qzxwvtnp$ having another representation, for $hjgrksla = 2, \dots, 2016$, we must have
\[
qzxwvtnp \not\equiv \begin{cases} hjgrksla/2 & hjgrksla \equiv 0 \pmod{2} \\ 0 & hjgrksla \equiv 1 \pmod{2}. \end{cases}
\]
Consequently, $qzxwvtnp \not\equiv 0 \pmod{dfhjklqw}$ for any odd prime $dfhjklqw<2017$ and $qzxwvtnp \equiv 0 \pmod{1024}$. Since $qzxwvtnp$ must be divisible by 2017, this again yields the claimed value of $plmdqrfz$.

kernel_variant.question:
A positive integer N is called bi-unique if it can be represented as a sum of k consecutive positive integers  

  N = a + (a+1) + \ldots  + (a+k-1)   (k > 1)                                           (\star )

for exactly the two distinct lengths  

  k_1 = 2017  and  k_2 = 2017^2 = 4 068 289,

and for no other value of k greater than 1.  
For every such N write  

  a_1 - the first term in its 2017-term decomposition,  
  a_2 - the first term in its 4 068 289-term decomposition.  

Determine  

  min { a_1 , a_2 }  

taken over all bi-unique integers N.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write a decomposition in the form (\star ).  
The sum of k consecutive integers equals k(2a+k-1)/2, whence  

  2N = k(2a+k-1).                      (1)

Conversely, every factorisation 2N = km with  

  * k > 1,  * k and m of opposite parity,  * m \geq  k+1        (2)

produces, and is produced by, a representation (\star ) with first term  
a = (m-k+1)/2.

Fix, once and for all, a bi-unique integer N.  
Put  

  m := 2a+k-1 > k, so that 2N = km.               (3)



1.  The two prescribed decompositions  
For k_1 = 2017 and k_2 = 2017^2 write  

  2N = 2017\cdot X = 2017^2\cdot Y, where X = 2a_1+2016, Y = 2a_2+2017^2-1.  (4)

Both 2017 and 2017^2 are odd; by (2) this forces X and Y to be even.  
Because 2017 is prime, (4) implies  

  X = 2017 Y,  Y = 2^\alpha  s  with \alpha  \geq  1, s odd.          (5)

Thus  

  2N = 2017^2 \cdot  2^\alpha  \cdot  s                       (6)



2.  Eliminating the factor 2017 from s  
Suppose, for contradiction, that 2017 divides s; write s = 2017 t with t odd.  
Equation (6) then becomes  

  2N = 2017^3 \cdot  2^\alpha  \cdot  t.                      (7)

Consider the two factors  

  u = 2^\alpha   (even),  v = 2017^3 t  (odd).  

They are of opposite parity; set  

  k := min{u, v},  m := max{u, v}.  

Both k and m exceed 1, and because u \neq  v we have m \geq  k+1.  
Consequently (k,m) satisfies (2), producing a decomposition of length k.  
This k is neither 2017 nor 2017^2 (it is even if k=u, or at least 2017^3 if k=v).  
Hence a third decomposition exists - impossible for a bi-unique N.  
Therefore  

  2017 \nmid  s.                         (8)



3.  Proving that the odd factor s equals 1  

Write 2N again as in (6) with (8) in force.

Case A.  s is composite.  
Let p be its smallest odd prime divisor (p \geq  3, p \neq  2017).  
Choose  

  k = 2017 p   (odd divisor of 2N).            (9)

Its partner equals  

  m = 2N/k = 2017 \cdot  2^\alpha  \cdot  (s/p),  an even integer.      (10)

Because s/p \geq  p and \alpha  \geq  1,

  m \geq  2017\cdot 2\cdot p > 2017 p = k.               (11)

Thus another legitimate decomposition of length k (\neq 2017,2017^2) appears - contradiction.  
Hence s cannot be composite.

Case B.  s is prime (by (8) necessarily s \neq  2017).  
We first show that the forthcoming exclusion of even lengths demands  

  2^\alpha  > s.                         (12)

Indeed, let u be any odd divisor of 2017^2 s.  
A potential even length has the form k = 2^\rho  u (\rho  \geq  1); applying (1)-(2) as before yields the necessity  

  2017^2 s/u < 2^\alpha  u + 1.              (13)

Putting u = 1 gives 2^\alpha  > 2017^2 s \geq  s, proving (12).

Now select  

  k = 2017 s   (odd divisor of 2N).           (14)

Its partner is m = 2017\cdot 2^\alpha  (even), and because of (12) we have m > k.  
Condition (2) is fulfilled, yielding a third decomposition - contradiction.  
Therefore Case B is impossible and we conclude  

  s = 1.                         (15)

Consequently  

  2N = 2017^2 \cdot  2^\alpha   with \alpha  \geq  1.             (16)



4.  Barring even lengths - a lower bound for \alpha   

Let k be any even divisor of 2N; write k = 2^\rho  u with u odd, \rho  \geq  1.  
From (16),

  m = 2N/k = 2017^2 \cdot  2^{\alpha -\rho }/u.            (17)

Opposite parity forces \alpha  = \rho , so that  

  (m, k) = (2017^2/u, 2^\alpha  u).  

Condition (2) requires m \geq  k+1, i.e.  

  2017^2/u \geq  2^\alpha  u + 1.               (18)

The left-hand side decreases and the right-hand side increases with u;  
the most difficult case is u = 1, giving  

  2017^2 \geq  2^\alpha  + 1.                 (19)

Numerically, 2^{21} = 2 097 152 < 2017^2 = 4 068 289 < 2^{22} = 4 194 304,  
so (19) fails for \alpha  \geq  22 and holds for \alpha  \leq  21.  Therefore

  \alpha  \geq  22 is necessary and sufficient to eliminate every even length. (20)



5.  Verifying that \alpha  \geq  22 indeed yields bi-uniqueness  

With s = 1, the odd divisors of 2N are 1, 2017, 2017^2 only.  
Thus the only possible odd lengths are k = 2017 and k = 2017^2.  
By (20) no even length survives when \alpha  \geq  22, so exactly the two required decompositions remain, and N is bi-unique.



6.  Computing a_1 and a_2  

For any \alpha  \geq  22 formulae (4)-(5) give  

  Y = 2^\alpha ,  X = 2017 Y = 2017\cdot 2^\alpha .  

Hence  

  a_2 = (Y - 2017^2 + 1)/2 = (2^\alpha  - 4 068 289 + 1)/2,  

  a_1 = (X - 2017 + 1)/2 = (2017\cdot 2^\alpha  - 2017 + 1)/2.     (21)

Because 2^\alpha  \leq  2017\cdot 2^\alpha  - 2017 + 1 for \alpha  \geq  1, we always have a_2 \leq  a_1,  
so  

  min{a_1, a_2} = a_2.                  (22)

The right-hand side is strictly increasing in \alpha , hence its minimum occurs at the minimal admissible exponent \alpha  = 22.  Substituting 2^{22} = 4 194 304,

  a_2(min) = (4 194 304 - 4 068 289 + 1)/2 = 63 008.    (23)

The corresponding integer  

  N_0 = 2017^2 \cdot  2^{21}                    (24)

indeed possesses exactly the two decompositions (k = 2017 and k = 2017^2) and no others.

Answer: 63 008.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Multiple interacting representations.  
Unlike the original problem, which involved a single length of 2017, the enhanced variant imposes two interacting lengths 2017 and 2017².  Their coexistence forces a non-trivial simultaneous factorisation (equation (2)) that has to be analysed via unique factorisation.

2.  Odd-versus-even length dichotomy.  
Proving that no even length can occur demands a delicate parity argument using the inequality e > k from Step 1; this is entirely absent from the original task.

3.  Control of the full divisor structure.  
The solver must show that having exactly two admissible lengths forces N to possess precisely one odd prime squared in its factorisation (Step 2) and then check every possible divisor of 2N (Step 3).  This uses deeper number-theoretic tools (divisor classification, size estimates of powers of two) than the original solution.

4.  Optimisation under an inequality constraint.  
Determining the minimal t that blocks all even lengths (inequality (5)) and still keeps both required starting integers positive adds an additional optimisation layer.

5.  Size handling.  
Explicit numbers on the order of four million (2017²) and four billion (a₁) must be managed accurately, increasing computational and conceptual load.

Altogether these ingredients demand substantially more theory (parity arguments for all divisors, simultaneous Diophantine conditions) and longer chains of reasoning than the prime-and-power-of-two trick used in the original problem.

original_kernel_variant.question:
A positive integer N is called bi-unique if it can be represented as a sum of k consecutive positive integers  

  N = a + (a+1) + \ldots  + (a+k-1)   (k > 1)                                           (\star )

for exactly the two distinct lengths  

  k_1 = 2017  and  k_2 = 2017^2 = 4 068 289,

and for no other value of k greater than 1.  
For every such N write  

  a_1 - the first term in its 2017-term decomposition,  
  a_2 - the first term in its 4 068 289-term decomposition.  

Determine  

  min { a_1 , a_2 }  

taken over all bi-unique integers N.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write a decomposition in the form (\star ).  
The sum of k consecutive integers equals k(2a+k-1)/2, whence  

  2N = k(2a+k-1).                      (1)

Conversely, every factorisation 2N = km with  

  * k > 1,  * k and m of opposite parity,  * m \geq  k+1        (2)

produces, and is produced by, a representation (\star ) with first term  
a = (m-k+1)/2.

Fix, once and for all, a bi-unique integer N.  
Put  

  m := 2a+k-1 > k, so that 2N = km.               (3)



1.  The two prescribed decompositions  
For k_1 = 2017 and k_2 = 2017^2 write  

  2N = 2017\cdot X = 2017^2\cdot Y, where X = 2a_1+2016, Y = 2a_2+2017^2-1.  (4)

Both 2017 and 2017^2 are odd; by (2) this forces X and Y to be even.  
Because 2017 is prime, (4) implies  

  X = 2017 Y,  Y = 2^\alpha  s  with \alpha  \geq  1, s odd.          (5)

Thus  

  2N = 2017^2 \cdot  2^\alpha  \cdot  s                       (6)



2.  Eliminating the factor 2017 from s  
Suppose, for contradiction, that 2017 divides s; write s = 2017 t with t odd.  
Equation (6) then becomes  

  2N = 2017^3 \cdot  2^\alpha  \cdot  t.                      (7)

Consider the two factors  

  u = 2^\alpha   (even),  v = 2017^3 t  (odd).  

They are of opposite parity; set  

  k := min{u, v},  m := max{u, v}.  

Both k and m exceed 1, and because u \neq  v we have m \geq  k+1.  
Consequently (k,m) satisfies (2), producing a decomposition of length k.  
This k is neither 2017 nor 2017^2 (it is even if k=u, or at least 2017^3 if k=v).  
Hence a third decomposition exists - impossible for a bi-unique N.  
Therefore  

  2017 \nmid  s.                         (8)



3.  Proving that the odd factor s equals 1  

Write 2N again as in (6) with (8) in force.

Case A.  s is composite.  
Let p be its smallest odd prime divisor (p \geq  3, p \neq  2017).  
Choose  

  k = 2017 p   (odd divisor of 2N).            (9)

Its partner equals  

  m = 2N/k = 2017 \cdot  2^\alpha  \cdot  (s/p),  an even integer.      (10)

Because s/p \geq  p and \alpha  \geq  1,

  m \geq  2017\cdot 2\cdot p > 2017 p = k.               (11)

Thus another legitimate decomposition of length k (\neq 2017,2017^2) appears - contradiction.  
Hence s cannot be composite.

Case B.  s is prime (by (8) necessarily s \neq  2017).  
We first show that the forthcoming exclusion of even lengths demands  

  2^\alpha  > s.                         (12)

Indeed, let u be any odd divisor of 2017^2 s.  
A potential even length has the form k = 2^\rho  u (\rho  \geq  1); applying (1)-(2) as before yields the necessity  

  2017^2 s/u < 2^\alpha  u + 1.              (13)

Putting u = 1 gives 2^\alpha  > 2017^2 s \geq  s, proving (12).

Now select  

  k = 2017 s   (odd divisor of 2N).           (14)

Its partner is m = 2017\cdot 2^\alpha  (even), and because of (12) we have m > k.  
Condition (2) is fulfilled, yielding a third decomposition - contradiction.  
Therefore Case B is impossible and we conclude  

  s = 1.                         (15)

Consequently  

  2N = 2017^2 \cdot  2^\alpha   with \alpha  \geq  1.             (16)



4.  Barring even lengths - a lower bound for \alpha   

Let k be any even divisor of 2N; write k = 2^\rho  u with u odd, \rho  \geq  1.  
From (16),

  m = 2N/k = 2017^2 \cdot  2^{\alpha -\rho }/u.            (17)

Opposite parity forces \alpha  = \rho , so that  

  (m, k) = (2017^2/u, 2^\alpha  u).  

Condition (2) requires m \geq  k+1, i.e.  

  2017^2/u \geq  2^\alpha  u + 1.               (18)

The left-hand side decreases and the right-hand side increases with u;  
the most difficult case is u = 1, giving  

  2017^2 \geq  2^\alpha  + 1.                 (19)

Numerically, 2^{21} = 2 097 152 < 2017^2 = 4 068 289 < 2^{22} = 4 194 304,  
so (19) fails for \alpha  \geq  22 and holds for \alpha  \leq  21.  Therefore

  \alpha  \geq  22 is necessary and sufficient to eliminate every even length. (20)



5.  Verifying that \alpha  \geq  22 indeed yields bi-uniqueness  

With s = 1, the odd divisors of 2N are 1, 2017, 2017^2 only.  
Thus the only possible odd lengths are k = 2017 and k = 2017^2.  
By (20) no even length survives when \alpha  \geq  22, so exactly the two required decompositions remain, and N is bi-unique.



6.  Computing a_1 and a_2  

For any \alpha  \geq  22 formulae (4)-(5) give  

  Y = 2^\alpha ,  X = 2017 Y = 2017\cdot 2^\alpha .  

Hence  

  a_2 = (Y - 2017^2 + 1)/2 = (2^\alpha  - 4 068 289 + 1)/2,  

  a_1 = (X - 2017 + 1)/2 = (2017\cdot 2^\alpha  - 2017 + 1)/2.     (21)

Because 2^\alpha  \leq  2017\cdot 2^\alpha  - 2017 + 1 for \alpha  \geq  1, we always have a_2 \leq  a_1,  
so  

  min{a_1, a_2} = a_2.                  (22)

The right-hand side is strictly increasing in \alpha , hence its minimum occurs at the minimal admissible exponent \alpha  = 22.  Substituting 2^{22} = 4 194 304,

  a_2(min) = (4 194 304 - 4 068 289 + 1)/2 = 63 008.    (23)

The corresponding integer  

  N_0 = 2017^2 \cdot  2^{21}                    (24)

indeed possesses exactly the two decompositions (k = 2017 and k = 2017^2) and no others.

Answer: 63 008.

1.  Multiple interacting representations.  
Unlike the original problem, which involved a single length of 2017, the enhanced variant imposes two interacting lengths 2017 and 2017².  Their coexistence forces a non-trivial simultaneous factorisation (equation (2)) that has to be analysed via unique factorisation.

2.  Odd-versus-even length dichotomy.  
Proving that no even length can occur demands a delicate parity argument using the inequality e > k from Step 1; this is entirely absent from the original task.

3.  Control of the full divisor structure.  
The solver must show that having exactly two admissible lengths forces N to possess precisely one odd prime squared in its factorisation (Step 2) and then check every possible divisor of 2N (Step 3).  This uses deeper number-theoretic tools (divisor classification, size estimates of powers of two) than the original solution.

4.  Optimisation under an inequality constraint.  
Determining the minimal t that blocks all even lengths (inequality (5)) and still keeps both required starting integers positive adds an additional optimisation layer.

5.  Size handling.  
Explicit numbers on the order of four million (2017²) and four billion (a₁) must be managed accurately, increasing computational and conceptual load.

Altogether these ingredients demand substantially more theory (parity arguments for all divisors, simultaneous Diophantine conditions) and longer chains of reasoning than the prime-and-power-of-two trick used in the original problem.
